title: Motor Oil on Cotton
stainChemistry: Classification: Oil/Grease (lipid-based). Motor oil is a complex mixture of petroleum hydrocarbons (C15-C50 alkanes, cycloalkanes, aromatics) plus additives (zinc dialkyldithiophosphate anti-wear, dispersants, detergents, viscosity modifiers). Much heavier and more tenacious than cooking oil. Contains carbon black particulate from combustion. Oil stains penetrate fiber structure through capillary action and hydrophobic affinity. Treatment: dry-side solvent (POG) to dissolve lipid phase, followed by surfactant emulsification to remove residual oil.
whyThisWorks: Automotive fluids are petroleum-based oils that soak in deep and resist water-based cleaners. Cotton absorbs liquids quickly and holds stains deep in the weave. NSD or a degreaser breaks down the oil so it becomes water-soluble and can be rinsed away.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply POG directly to the oil stain. POG is a volatile dry solvent with strong affinity for lipids — it dissolves oil droplets on contact. Tamp gently and blot with absorbent cloth. Dwell 5–10 minutes.
2. If POG plateaus, apply amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. Blot with clean absorbent cloth between applications. Never use on acetate fabrics. Dwell 3–5 minutes.
3. Apply NSD to emulsify any remaining oil micro-droplets and remove solvent residue. Rinse thoroughly with cool water. Dwell 5 minutes.
4. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to restore fiber to its natural state. Blot dry with clean cloth and allow to air dry. Dwell 1–2 minutes.
professionalProtocol.temperature: warm for rinsing — warm water helps emulsify residual oil; use solvent phase first
warnings: Rubbing instead of blotting — spreads the stain
Not testing in an inconspicuous area first
Using too aggressive a chemical without checking surface compatibility

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain. Wait 5 minutes.
2. Brush off the powder with a clean white cloth.
3. Put a drop of Dawn NSD on the stain. Blot it in.
4. Let it sit for 2–3 minutes. Rinse with cold water.
5. Repeat if needed. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Try a dry cleaning solvent (Carbona Stain Devil #5 or Goo Gone). If the stain survives two rounds, a professional cleaner can use concentrated agents and specialized equipment.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing pushes the stain deeper into the fabric.
Never add water to an oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, and you'll spread it.
Avoid heat until the stain is completely gone.

neverDo: Apply heat before oil is removed — polymerizes petroleum permanently into fiber
Add water to a fresh oil stain first — spreads it before any treatment can work
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates most agents including bleach on whites. For colored cotton, test peroxide first. High heat can set petroleum stains permanently.

escalation.when: Motor oil stain remains after full solvent protocol — likely old or polymerized by heat
escalation.whatToTell: Motor oil stain on cotton, petroleum-based. Treated at home with NSD. Please use professional dry-side solvent protocol with POG.

contextQuestions: Is the stain fresh or has it been sitting and drying?
Has the garment been through the dryer?
What type of oil — clean motor oil, dirty used oil, or transmission fluid?
Is the cotton white or colored?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Dirty used motor oil contains carbon black and combustion byproducts — harder to remove than clean oil. Dryer exposure polymerizes the oil and makes removal very difficult. On colored cotton, test all solvents on a hidden seam first.
